title: Projects
text:
This page presents three projects that reflect my interests in business analysis, marketing strategy, supply chain thinking, and process improvement. Together, they show how I approach business problems from both a strategic and operational perspective.






Park & Polish was developed as part of a New Enterprise Development project. The concept focused on turning parked time into productive service time by offering a mobile car wash and detailing service while customers’ vehicles were already parked at locations such as golf clubs, airports, and business parks.



Traditional car washing is often inconvenient, time-consuming, and poorly aligned with the routines of busy customers. Our research identified that many users delay vehicle cleaning because they do not want to spend extra time driving to a car wash or waiting in queues. Park & Polish addressed this by repositioning car care as a service delivered during existing parking time, especially in environments where cars remain idle for several hours.



The objective of this project was to develop a venture concept that combined convenience, sustainability, and digital service design into a differentiated business model. The aim was not simply to create another car wash service, but to design a customer-centred solution with clear growth potential and a premium brand identity.



My contribution focused on supporting the development of the business concept and strategic logic behind the service. I worked on understanding customer pain points, clarifying the value proposition, and shaping how convenience, digital experience, and repeat-use mechanisms could support long-term growth. I also contributed to presenting the service as a scalable and customer-focused solution rather than a one-off transaction.



This project drew on several business and marketing tools, including:

customer pain point analysis
empathy mapping
strategy canvas thinking
value proposition development
business model and growth planning
KPI-based marketing evaluation

The project materials also incorporated digital journey design, partnership logic, and retention strategy through memberships, referrals, and service quality systems.







Several insights shaped the final concept:

Convenience as competitive advantage: the business turned “lost” parking time into useful service time.
Digital experience as differentiation: booking, payment, notifications, and loyalty features created a more seamless customer journey than traditional car washes.
Sustainability as part of the brand: the concept used water-saving methods and biodegradable products to align with customer expectations and environmental priorities.
Scalability through partnerships: partner venues such as clubs, airports, and parking facilities were central to both customer acquisition and trust-building.
Retention and quality systems matter: memberships, referrals, before/after proof, reviews, and KPI dashboards were used to support repeat business and long-term growth.




The final outcome was a professionally structured venture concept with a clear value proposition, customer segment, service model, and growth strategy. The project showed how a familiar service could be redesigned through customer insight, digital thinking, and strategic positioning to create a more compelling business opportunity.



This project strengthened my understanding of customer-centred business design, value proposition development, and marketing strategy. It also showed me that a strong business idea depends not only on creativity, but on how effectively it solves a real user problem and connects that solution to operations, partnerships, and measurable growth.








This project focused on the analysis of a cookie manufacturing company, with the aim of improving both production and sales performance through better cost understanding and process evaluation.



Manufacturing businesses often face pressure to manage rising input costs, maintain margins, and improve efficiency across multiple stages of production and delivery. In this project, the key challenge was to understand where costs were being generated, how they affected profit margins, and where operational improvements could create better financial outcomes.



The objective was to evaluate the company’s production and sales processes, identify inefficiencies, and generate practical recommendations to improve profitability. This included a focus on production costs, margin performance, and cost-saving opportunities across the supply chain.



I took lead responsibility for calculating production costs, analysing profit margins, and identifying opportunities to reduce costs. My work involved reviewing how ingredients, labour, and operational activities contributed to the overall cost structure and how those costs influenced business performance.



The project used a structured business analysis approach, including:

cost-benefit analysis
process mapping
profitability analysis
supply chain review
operational stage evaluation from sourcing to delivery

These methods helped break down the business into manageable stages and made it easier to identify where improvements could have the greatest impact.



The project highlighted that profitability is shaped not only by sales volume, but also by the relationship between production cost, process efficiency, and operational control. A business may appear commercially active while still losing value through waste, unnecessary expense, or inefficient workflows. This analysis reinforced the importance of examining both financial and process data together when making business recommendations.



The final recommendations aimed to:

improve production efficiency
reduce unnecessary cost across the supply chain
strengthen margin performance
support more sustainable profitability

This project strengthened my ability to connect operational data, financial analysis, and process evaluation in order to identify practical improvements for business performance.



This experience helped me develop stronger analytical thinking, financial interpretation, and problem-solving skills. It also reinforced the value of structured business analysis in real decision-making. Rather than looking at isolated problems, I learned how to evaluate an operation as a connected system where cost, process, and profitability all influence each other.





This project presents a 3D circular supply chain model created to explain material inputs, production, customer use, reverse logistics, repair, remanufacturing, recycling, and waste minimisation loops.



Circular economy principles
Reverse logistics and recovery loops
Repair, reuse, recycling, and remanufacturing
Operational thinking in sustainable supply chain design





  

Watch on YouTube



This project helped me understand circular supply chains in a more practical and visual way. By designing the model, I was able to connect theoretical concepts such as reverse flows, waste minimisation, and stakeholder coordination with a real operational structure. It also strengthened my ability to communicate supply chain ideas creatively and clearly through both design and presentation. pbpaste >> projects.qmd




As I continue developing this portfolio, I plan to extend the analytical side of my professional project further by adding examples of SQL queries, Python-based analysis, and supporting visualisations. This will allow me to present not only the business logic behind my work, but also the technical and analytical tools I am developing through my studies, which is important for aligning the portfolio with the assignment requirements.

title: About
text:
I am a final-year Business Studies student at Dublin City University, with a strong academic focus on business analytics, marketing planning, and commercial problem-solving. Alongside my studies, I am currently working as an Accounting Assistant Intern, where I support day-to-day finance and administrative processes while gaining practical experience in financial documentation, organisation, and business operations.
I am also pursuing ACCA studies, which are strengthening my understanding of accounting, finance, and professional practice. Through my education, internship experience, and project work, I have built a solid foundation in business analysis, structured thinking, and decision-making based on evidence and business objectives.
My main professional interests lie in business analysis, marketing strategy, process improvement, and graduate roles where I can continue to develop analytical, strategic, and communication skills. I am particularly interested in projects that combine commercial insight with practical recommendations to improve performance, customer value, and operational efficiency.


<img src="images/about-photo.jpg" alt="Sophia Sufei Chen personal photo">

title: Reflection
text: Building this portfolio became more than a design task. It was a process of reviewing my academic journey, solving practical web problems, and learning how presentation, structure, and content work together in a professional digital space.

title: Reflection
section: What I learned from creating this portfolio
text:
What I learned from creating this portfolio
Developing this portfolio helped me think more critically about how academic work can be presented in a professional, personal, and accessible way. Rather than simply listing modules or assignments, I had to decide how each section should represent my strengths, interests, and future direction. This process made me reflect not only on the content of my work, but also on how design, structure, and presentation shape the impression a viewer receives.
Through building this website, I became more aware of the connection between business analysis, communication, and digital presentation. My academic projects, particularly in enterprise development, process improvement, and supply chain thinking, showed me that strong ideas need to be supported by clear structure, evidence, and practical recommendation. Translating those ideas into a portfolio format encouraged me to present them more selectively and more thoughtfully.

title: Reflection
text:
Challenges I faced during development
One of the biggest challenges I encountered was getting the layout to behave consistently across different pages. At several points, when I adjusted the homepage or projects page, the same style rules unexpectedly affected the about page or reflection page. This showed me that web design requires not only creativity, but also accuracy and control. A small visual change could have a wider impact than expected, so I needed to test each adjustment carefully.
Another challenge was editing files through the terminal. Because many updates were made through command-based editing rather than directly using a visual editor, I had to be especially careful with formatting and structure. At times, pasted content was inserted in the wrong place, long commands were affected by quoting issues, or blocks were displayed as raw code instead of being rendered properly. These mistakes were frustrating, but they also taught me to work more patiently and to check each step before moving on.
I also experienced difficulties with GitHub deployment. There were moments when the local preview looked correct, but the online version did not refresh immediately. This forced me to understand the difference between source files, rendered output, and the deployed docs folder used by GitHub Pages. It helped me realise that publishing a website is not only about design – it also depends on file structure, deployment settings, and version control.

title: Reflection
text:
Reflection on problem-solving
Although the process was sometimes difficult, it improved my confidence in solving technical problems in a structured way. I had to identify which file or style rule was causing a problem, test smaller changes, review the result, and then refine the solution without damaging the rest of the site. This felt very similar to business problem-solving, where a challenge often needs to be broken down, tested carefully, and improved through iteration.
I also learned the value of patience and persistence. At several points, a design update that seemed simple created unexpected problems elsewhere in the site. Instead of abandoning the idea, I had to review previous versions, compare outcomes, and make gradual improvements. This reminded me that professional results often come through refinement rather than speed. It also reinforced the importance of accuracy, especially when working with digital tools that are sensitive to small structural errors.

title: Reflection
text:
Personal growth and future direction
This portfolio process also helped me think more clearly about my future development. It confirmed my interest in areas that combine analysis, communication, and structured presentation. Business analysis, marketing planning, process improvement, and operations-related work all require the ability to organise information clearly and present ideas in a way that others can understand and act upon. Creating this website gave me a chance to practise those skill in a practical and visible format.
Looking ahead, I would like to continue improving both the content and technical quality of the site. I want to present future projects with stronger visual storytelling, clearer professional structure, and more confidence in how I communicate ideas online. I also hope to build a better understanding of digital presentation tools, because I now see them not only as technical skills, but as a part of professional communication.

title: Reflection
text:
Key takeaways

  
    What I learned
    I learned that strong presentation improves the clarity, credibility, and professionalism of academic work.
  
  
    Challenges faced
    I dealt with layout conflicts, terminal editing issues, and deployment problems during development.
  
  
    Future growth
    I want to continue strengthening the balance between analytical thinking, digital presentation, and professional communication.
  

Overall, creating this portfolio has been a meaningful learning experience. It allowed me to reflect on my academic journey, recognise the importance of design and usability, and develop a clearer understanding of how content, structure, and presentation work together. More importantly, it showed me that presenting my work effectively is not only about appearance. It is about clarity, intention, and the ability to communicate who I am, what I have learned, and what I can contribute.

title: Skill Preview
text:
A refined look at the strengths behind my portfolio — how I think, work, communicate, and grow through academic learning, project practice, and professional preparation.
My interests are rooted in business analysis, marketing planning, process improvement, and clear communication. I enjoy turning complexity into structure, and ideas into something practical, polished, and meaningful.




Structured thinking

Analytical mindset

Clear communication

Business curiosity

Continuous growth








I enjoy understanding how information connects, where problems begin, and how insight can support better decisions. I am especially drawn to structured analysis, interpretation, and translating findings into practical action.



I like exploring how customer needs, communication, and strategy come together. Marketing, to me, is most interesting when creativity is supported by logic, positioning, and clear value creation.



My learning in accounting and ACCA-related areas has strengthened my financial awareness, attention to detail, and confidence in business fundamentals. It has helped me become more disciplined, precise, and professionally grounded.



I am interested in how work can become more efficient, smoother, and better organised. I enjoy noticing where improvements can be made and thinking in ways that make systems clearer and more effective.








I prefer to break large ideas into manageable parts, identify the core issue, and approach tasks with structure and calm focus.



I value communication that is clear, respectful, and purposeful — whether in teamwork, presentation, or written work.



I am still building my path, and I see that as a strength. Each project, challenge, and experience teaches me something I can carry forward.








I start by understanding context, expectations, and the real purpose behind a task.



I like bringing order to ideas — grouping, simplifying, and building a clearer direction.



I focus on outcomes that feel clear, polished, and useful rather than rushed or superficial.






I hope to continue developing in business-focused graduate roles where analysis, communication, customer understanding, and improvement-minded thinking can create real value. My goal is not only to build stronger technical skills, but also to become someone who contributes with clarity, curiosity, and professionalism.

In this project, the key challenge was to understand where costs were being generated, how they affected profit margins, and where operational improvements could create better financial outcomes.



The objective was to evaluate the company’s production and sales processes, identify inefficiencies, and generate practical recommendations to improve profitability. This included a focus on production costs, margin performance, and cost-saving opportunities across the supply chain.



I took lead responsibility for calculating production costs, analysing profit margins, and identifying opportunities to reduce costs. My work involved reviewing how ingredients, labour, and operational activities contributed to the overall cost structure and how those costs influenced business performance.



The project used a structured business analysis approach, including:

cost-benefit analysis
process mapping
profitability analysis
supply chain review
operational stage evaluation from sourcing to delivery

These methods helped break down the business into manageable stages and made it easier to identify where improvements could have the greatest impact.



The project highlighted that profitability is shaped not only by sales volume, but also by the relationship between production cost, process efficiency, and operational control. A business may appear commercially active while still losing value through waste, unnecessary expense, or inefficient workflows. This analysis reinforced the importance of examining both financial and process data together when making business recommendations.



The final recommendations aimed to:

improve production efficiency
reduce unnecessary cost across the supply chain
strengthen margin performance
support more sustainable profitability

This project strengthened my ability to connect operational data, financial analysis, and process evaluation in order to identify practical improvements for business performance.



This experience helped me develop stronger analytical thinking, financial interpretation, and problem-solving skills. It also reinforced the value of structured business analysis in real decision-making. Rather than looking at isolated problems, I learned how to evaluate an operation as a connected system where cost, process, and profitability all influence each other.

title: Projects
text: As I continue developing this portfolio, I plan to extend the analytical side of my professional project further by adding examples of SQL queries, Python-based analysis, and supporting visualisations. This will allow me to present not only the business logic behind my work, but also the technical and analytical tools I am developing through my studies, which is important for aligning the portfolio with the assignment requirements.

title: About
text:
A final-year Business Studies student with a growing passion for business analysis, marketing planning, and process improvement.
I am currently studying Business Studies at Dublin City University and building practical experience through academic projects, internship exposure, and ACCA learning. I enjoy structured thinking, solving real business problems, and connecting analysis with meaningful decisions.
My interests lie in business insight, operational efficiency, customer-focused strategy, and graduate opportunities where I can continue to grow both professionally and personally.
